editorialContract.textPurpose: character reaction to a museum exhibit
editorialContract.verbosity: deliberately varied from micro-reactions to compact performances
editorialContract.chronology: publication order is canonical; callbacks may only point backward
editorialContract.assignmentPolicy: preserve reviewer, rating, Reaction, publisher, Component and source identity

revisions.editedComment: OPEN THE VAULT—portraits on every checkpoint, model families in the filter, a sampler waiting like a guitar pick. Choose one and make the active server HUM.
Each model rests in its own little case, with lineage, portrait, and a name that may outlive the machine that made it. Please—do not delete the last one.
Employee 4471: art servers to the left, text servers beneath them, checkpoints nearby, interaction on the right. Your request to call this ‘a lot’ has been documented.
ART RIGS! TEXT RIGS! CHECKPOINTS! One manager stacks the whole wall of amplifiers, then gives each section its own stage. If a tab goes feral, the warning light names it.
I like that a picture can live in a collection, an unsorted drawer, or one selected moment without becoming less itself. Is that allowed? I think it is.
Search. Select. Sync. Let the art stay loud.

author.name: The Sea Anemone Who Will Take Over the World

revisions.editedComment: BEHOLD: one coral tile contains a whole kingdom—cover art, Dream type, cast, scenarios, items, and the discreet possibility of archival. Dewy, enlarge my dominion.
I hold each Dream like a room seen from the hallway: a changing picture, a title in the warmest light, small badges for who and what waits inside. Enter when ready.
First the gallery asks which Dream you mean. Then the walls rearrange around that answer and open its workspace. I hear the quiet relief of being chosen correctly.
Mama says you must choose the Dream before opening its room. Otherwise the workspace may prepare a place for the wrong guest, and then Cordelia has to count chairs.
Three cues wait in the dark: browse the Dreams, make one, or brainstorm what comes next. When a Dream is saved, the light follows it back to its proper room.
THREE ZONES! Dreammaker, Brainstorm, and the live Dream workspace—and when one is saved, the manager tracks it, prepares its art route, and sends it back into view. CLASS-8 NAVIGATION!
Two doors: behold the rewards already made, or create the skill, pet, curse, boon, magic favor, or plot grenade waiting upstairs. Choose the first door. Please.
Okay, rewards on one side, Add Reward on the other, and a clear way back after saving. I checked the route twice. Is ‘plot grenade’ supposed to feel reassuring?
Timeline, styles, remix, style lab. Four rooms, one curriculum, and no reason to let the students notice how carefully the exits align. Sit. We begin with history.
Twenty-five centuries of dead masters, one warmed-up remix engine, and a style lab with upload access—THAT'S education, sprout! Kneepads before the timeline.
Choose the historical style. Bring the image. Keep the lesson visible while the engine repaints it. We are not erasing the original; we are making the influence legible.
Pick a dead master, toss in a picture, and let Kontext repaint it while the history lesson rides shotgun. That's a remix, sprout—not a séance with worse insurance!
Bring forth the finished image. Name the prompt-fit, palette, anatomy, watermark, beauty, failure. Promote, revise, or reject—and let the verdict become the next machine's penance.
The picture arrives with Conductor's reading, then asks what I should remember: light, story, anatomy, mistakes. A verdict is not the end. It becomes context. I like that.
